name: Retail & E-commerce
summary: Multi-channel selling, where the support conversation, the stock figure and the order record are the same event seen from three sides.
overview: Retail businesses rarely have a software problem. They have a data problem, a stock problem and a returns problem that software gets blamed for.

The pattern is consistent: each sales channel imposes its own rules on the catalogue, its own order states and its own service expectations, and the business ends up maintaining a translation layer made of people.
I work on this from both sides — as a consultant designing commerce and support processes, and as someone who runs an e-commerce business and has paid for these mistakes personally.
typicalProblems: Product data assembled per channel by hand
Stock figures that differ between the sales system and the warehouse
Returns, exchanges, defects and transit damage all handled as one process
Enquiries arriving on four channels and only some becoming records
Margin calculated on supplier price rather than landed cost
Order states that mean different things per channel

exampleEngagements.summary: Support transformation across messaging, social, email and phone, separating returns, exchanges, defects and transit damage into distinct outcomes with distinct liability.
Marketplace architecture covering merchant onboarding, catalogue approval, instalment checkout and payout.

architectureConsiderations: Decide which system owns stock quantity before anything else
The order state model must survive every channel, or you will maintain several
Returns need a stock disposition state, not a status
Support tooling must link to the order system rather than copy it
Landed cost belongs in inventory or accounting, never in a CRM
commonRisks: Stock accuracy is a discipline problem a system exposes rather than solves
Marketplace rules are non-negotiable and change without consultation
Reverse logistics is assumed to work like forward logistics
Category taxonomies grow until reporting is meaningless

name: Marketplaces & Reseller Businesses
summary: Multi-party commerce, where the hardest questions are commercial boundaries rather than features.
overview: A marketplace is not a shop with more suppliers. It is a set of relationships between parties who each have their own interests, and the system's job is to hold those boundaries.
Almost every genuinely difficult decision here — merchant approval, payout trigger, return liability, servicing responsibility — is commercial. The technology follows once someone has decided.
typicalProblems: Merchant onboarding by email and spreadsheet, with no verification standard
Payout trigger undefined until the first return after payment
Commission structures that differ per merchant and live in a spreadsheet
Third-party merchant orders following an entirely separate path
After-sales responsibility defaulting to whoever the customer calls
Manual approval as the growth ceiling

exampleEngagements.summary: Direct resale and third-party merchant models through one storefront, with instalment purchasing and an external finance provider.

architectureConsiderations: Model the payout trigger as a commercial decision with a financial exposure attached
Commission rules belong in configurable data, never in code
The financial platform owns the ledger; the marketplace holds state
Merchant suspension must be designed in from the start, not retrofitted
Manual approval is a legitimate choice — document its cost
commonRisks: Payout before the return window creates unrecoverable exposure
Merchant data quality becomes your data quality
Brand-protective manual approval caps throughput
Responsibility ambiguity between marketplace, merchant and provider lands on the customer

name: Fashion & Jewellery
summary: Product businesses where catalogue quality, channel rules and customer experience are the operation.
overview: I run an e-commerce and jewellery business. This is not project exposure — it is the operation I am accountable for.

What it teaches is unglamorous: product data is never as clean as a requirement assumes, marketplace channels do not negotiate their attribute rules, and returns are a permanent share of volume rather than an exception.
typicalProblems: Product attributes assembled differently for each marketplace
Images and copy blocking listings
Stock accuracy across channels
Returns and exchanges as a standing volume, not an edge case
Social and messaging channels carrying real support load with no record

exampleEngagements.summary: Direct operator experience across catalogue, listings, inventory, marketplace channels, fulfilment, support and marketing.
Support and returns transformation across four channels.

architectureConsiderations: Master product data needs one owner and one convention
Channel eligibility rules should be data, not judgement
Support on messaging channels needs to route into a record, not a phone
Returns need disposition states before they need a policy page
commonRisks: Marketplace attribute requirements change without notice
Enrichment quietly becomes an internal cost
Personal-phone support leaves no history
Promotions interact badly with partial refunds and tax

name: Financial Services & Lending
summary: Commerce with a lending decision inside it — where the architecture question is what you are not allowed to do.
overview: Instalment purchasing looks like a checkout feature. It is a licensed activity wearing a checkout feature's clothes.

The consistent work here is boundary-drawing: the lending decision stays with the provider, the ledger stays with the financial platform, and the commerce system holds state and orchestration. The temptation to drift across those lines by increment is the main risk.
typicalProblems: Business drifting toward building a credit engine it is not licensed to operate
Order confirmed before the finance decision returns
Undefined responsibility for repayment chasing versus servicing
No refund path for a financed purchase
Provider downtime with no fallback decision
Customers calling the wrong party and receiving no answer

exampleEngagements.summary: Instalment checkout with an external finance provider, with the lending decision held externally by design and written into scope as an exclusion.

architectureConsiderations: The lending decision belongs to the licensed party — write it in as an exclusion, not a deferral
Order confirmation must be gated on the provider's decision
The financial platform is the source of truth for money
Servicing and collections need a written responsibility split before launch
Access control on financial data is a compliance question
commonRisks: Regulatory exposure if lending logic drifts internal
Provider downtime blocking checkout
Refunds on financed orders involving a third party you do not control
Customers contacting the retailer about repayments regardless of the contract

name: Healthcare & Medical Services
summary: Service operations with field activity, where coordination and record-keeping carry more weight than pipeline.
overview: My involvement here has been on the operational and customer-service side — support processes, field activity, coordination between departments and communication workflows — rather than clinical systems.
The pattern that repeats: the operational record and the customer conversation are held in different places, and the person on the phone can see neither.
typicalProblems: Support enquiries with no link to the service record
Field activity coordinated by phone
Mileage and routing handled informally
Returns and servicing of equipment with no asset history
Department handoffs with no owner

exampleEngagements.summary: Customer-service operations, field activity, mileage and routing discussions, returns and servicing, and departmental communication workflows.

architectureConsiderations: Access control on personal data is a legal constraint, not a preference
Service history belongs against the asset, not the ticket
Field capacity needs modelling before scheduling can be automated
Communication workflows need a record, not a channel
commonRisks: Data sensitivity constrains design choices that would be routine elsewhere
Field connectivity is not guaranteed
Location tracking has employee-relations implications
Departmental boundaries are organisational, not technical

name: Education & Training
summary: Participant, assessor and event relationships that a sales-shaped CRM does not anticipate.
overview: Training organisations get sold CRM and then discover that a participant is not a lead, an assessment is not a deal, and a partial verification outcome has nowhere to go.
The work is usually modelling: what are the real objects, which of them a standard module can carry, and which need to exist in their own right.
typicalProblems: Different request types forced through one lifecycle
Events serving multiple requests with no representation of that
Assessor and trainer skills matched from memory
Verification outcomes that can be partial
Availability maintained manually and wrong by lunchtime
Multi-entity structures with shared participants

exampleEngagements.descriptor: South African early-childhood development sector organisation
exampleEngagements.summary: Study and training lifecycles, training events, assessor and competency modelling, and a verification lifecycle including partial outcomes.
Public enrolment, booking and payment portal over a structured operational process.

architectureConsiderations: Decide early which concepts are typed records and which need custom modules
The final positive state of each lifecycle is the question that turns a flow into a specification
Link records beat multi-selects when the relationship carries its own data
Form-submitted identifiers must be validated server-side
Public users need a portal, not a licence
commonRisks: Modelling everything as a deal produces a system nobody uses
Manual availability drifts, and pretending otherwise is worse
Portals get reintroduced by increment
Multi-entity fiscal and currency differences are assumed identical

name: Marine & Yacht Licensing
summary: The clearest relationship-modelling problem I have worked on: one asset, six concurrent company roles.
overview: A vessel is the asset, and the vessel is not the customer. Ownership, management, licensing, billing, brokerage and shipyard involvement are separate relationships that can sit against the same vessel at once, and each can change independently.
Standard account-contact models cannot express this. The engagement value was in refusing to flatten it.
typicalProblems: A single 'customer' field where six roles exist
The billing party assumed to be the owner
Role changes overwriting history rather than appending to it
Renewals depending on someone remembering
Contract terms living in prose so nothing downstream can act on them
Brokers and shipyards treated as customers

exampleEngagements.descriptor: European marine and yacht services business
exampleEngagements.summary: Vessel-centric data model with dated party-role relationships supporting six simultaneous company roles per vessel.

architectureConsiderations: Model parties independently of the roles they hold
Role relationships need effective and end dates or history is lost
The billing party must be explicit on every agreement
Currency, tax and fiscal rules belong in the accounting platform
History is a requirement, not a nice-to-have — 'who was responsible last year' is a real question
commonRisks: A missing role type discovered after build changes the model
Users reintroduce ambiguity by asking for a simple customer field
Migration from documents and spreadsheets is larger than it looks
Historical expectations exceeding the data that exists

name: Telecommunications
summary: Customer portals, service processes and multi-phase delivery, usually through a partner.
overview: My exposure here has been around customer-facing portals, service and sales workflows, and phased implementation in partner-led delivery models rather than network operations.
The recurring theme is subscription and service state: what the customer has, what they are entitled to, and whether the support desk can see either.
typicalProblems: Service entitlement invisible to the person answering the phone
Subscription changes pro-rated by hand
Portals delivered as a phase and then treated as the whole project
External systems owning data the CRM assumes it has
Multi-phase scope eroding under commercial pressure

exampleEngagements.summary: Customer portal, service and sales process work within a partner-led, multi-phase implementation.
Event-driven ticket creation from an external platform with validation, deduplication and failure handling.

architectureConsiderations: Entitlement must be visible where the conversation happens
Portals carry their own permission and support burden
Billing rules belong in the billing system
Phase boundaries need written entry and exit criteria to survive
commonRisks: Portal scope expanding into a second product
Involuntary churn from payment failures is larger than expected
External system dependencies not tracked as risks
Phase erosion

name: Manufacturing
summary: Quality, tooling and trial cycles — iterative processes that linear workflows cannot express.
overview: Manufacturing quality is a loop: first article, trial, adjustment, re-inspection, until the part is right. A system that records only the final state has recorded the least useful part.
The other constant is the paper form. It survived every previous system because it is fast. Any replacement that is slower will be bypassed, and the data will be worse than the paper.
typicalProblems: Quality checked at the end, where rework is most expensive
Inspection results on paper and filed
Trial cycles run informally
External departments and suppliers participating with no access
Root cause not linked to corrective action
Corrective actions closed rather than verified

exampleEngagements.descriptor: Manufacturing business with an internal tool room
exampleEngagements.summary: Quality control and tool-room processes, forms, agreements, trial cycles and external participation.

exampleEngagements.descriptor: South African B2B laboratory and instrumentation distributor
exampleEngagements.summary: CRM sales layer over retained inventory and accounting platforms.

architectureConsiderations: Model trial cycles as iterations, not approvals
Shop-floor capture speed is a requirement, not a usability preference
External parties contribute through forms, not licences
Verification, not closure, ends a corrective action
Segregation of failed material is a control, not a status
commonRisks: Digital capture slower than paper will be ignored
Root cause defaulting to a convenient answer
External participation depending on parties with no incentive
Trial complexity outgrowing the model

name: Energy & Utilities
summary: Accounts, contracts, utility records and supplier relationships — data structure work more than workflow work.
overview: My exposure here has been around commercial relationship and contract data: accounts, contacts, contracts, utility records and suppliers, and the operational data management around them.
The recurring problem is that the commercial relationship structure is more complex than a single account record admits.
typicalProblems: Contracts as PDFs with no extracted terms
Multiple parties per account with undefined roles
Utility records disconnected from the commercial relationship
Supplier terms not recorded anywhere actionable
Renewal and obligation dates unmonitored

exampleEngagements.summary: Accounts, contacts, contracts, utility records, suppliers and the commercial relationship structure around them.

architectureConsiderations: Capture key contract terms as structured data, not prose
Party roles need to be explicit where several parties exist
Obligation and date monitoring must be data-driven
Access control on commercial terms is a real requirement
commonRisks: Extracting terms from legacy contracts is a manual project
Party structure more complex than the account model allows
Regulatory reporting requirements arriving late

name: Mobility & Rental
summary: Assets on the move: availability, renewals, condition and compliance.
overview: Rental operations run on asset availability and condition, with renewal and compliance cycles that are dates in a system or reminders in a person's head.
Exposure here has covered rental management, inventory, renewals, customer onboarding and the coordination between warehouse and store.
typicalProblems: Availability maintained manually
Condition discovered by the next customer
Renewals depending on memory
Compliance dates in a calendar reminder
Asset custody untracked

exampleEngagements.summary: Rental management, inventory, renewals, vehicle processes, customer onboarding, warehouse and store coordination, UAT and training.
Booking, capacity and payment portal over a structured operational process.

architectureConsiderations: One source of availability, or none is trusted
Condition capture at handover and return, or liability is a conversation
Compliance dates need lead-time alerts
Custody as an event, not a field
commonRisks: Manual availability drift
Compliance requirements varying by jurisdiction
Register data quality at go-live
Condition disputes with no evidence

name: Automotive
summary: Vehicles, servicing and finance — asset identity and entitlement as the recurring problems.
overview: Exposure here has been around vehicle records, servicing, customer onboarding and the finance and instalment side of vehicle purchasing.
The pattern: the vehicle has an identity and a history, and most systems only record the transaction.
typicalProblems: The order recorded, the asset not
Warranty and entitlement invisible at the point of contact
Service history with the garage
Instalment and finance arrangements owned elsewhere with no visibility
Compliance dates untracked

exampleEngagements.summary: Vehicle records, servicing processes, customer onboarding and rental operations.
Instalment purchasing with an external finance provider.

architectureConsiderations: The asset needs an identity independent of the order
Service history attaches to the asset
Entitlement must be checkable before dispatch
Finance arrangements stay with the provider
commonRisks: Warranty data missing for historical sales
Asset registers built from unreliable spreadsheets
Servicing responsibility ambiguous across channels

name: Home Improvement & Installations
summary: Field work in someone's house: job types that genuinely differ, deposits, evidence and claims.
overview: Installation work sits between a ticket and a project. It has a quote, a deposit, a scheduled visit, a skilled resource, parts, a customer standing in their own home, and an invoice that should not wait for paperwork to come back from a van.
The recurring design failure is forcing genuinely different job types down one workflow because it is faster to build.
typicalProblems: Different job types forced through one blueprint
Scheduling by whiteboard with skills matched from memory
Deposit rules living in someone's head
Completion evidenced by photographs in a messaging group
Invoicing blocked by paperwork rather than by work
Claims classified case by case

exampleEngagements.summary: Field service, CRM, accounting and support as one operation, with three job types and three blueprints.
Claims process rebuild with a cascading product / claim / issue classification driving evidence, inspection and liability.

architectureConsiderations: Job type drives the workflow — one blueprint for three job types is a permanent workaround
Address validation and skill-based scheduling are never as small as they look at scoping
Completion evidence should release the invoice
Cascading classification belongs in a dependency table, not in code
External inspectors contribute through forms, not licences
commonRisks: Connectivity on site
Technicians reverting to the messaging group
Skill data incomplete at go-live
Scope absorbed as goodwill until the margin disappears

name: Property & Agent Services
summary: Agent-led sales processes where the commission model and the compliance environment shape the pipeline.
overview: Exposure here has been through review and blueprint work on agent-led sales processes, including gap analysis against changing commission and disclosure practice.
The pattern is that the pipeline is only half the process — the other half is compliance, disclosure and commission, and it is usually the half that is not modelled.
typicalProblems: Pipeline modelled without the compliance steps that actually gate it
Commission splits calculated by hand
Disclosure and documentation obligations untracked
Lead source attribution guessed
Agent activity invisible until the deal closes or does not

exampleEngagements.summary: Blueprint and gap review of an agent-led sales process, including findings against current commission practice.

architectureConsiderations: Compliance steps are stages, not paperwork
Commission rules as configurable data with version history
Document obligations need dates and owners
Attribution needs capture at the enquiry, not at the close
commonRisks: Regulatory practice changing mid-project
Commission rules embedded in code
Agent adoption where the alternative is a phone
Attribution as an argument rather than a measurement

name: Non-profit Organisations
summary: Volunteers, assets and incidents — an operation that is not a sales pipeline, run by people with no spare hours.
overview: Non-profits are routinely sold CRM as a donor tool and then asked to run their whole operation in it. Volunteers, equipment, vehicles, incidents and training currency have more in common with field operations than with fundraising.
The binding constraint is administrative capacity. A design that needs a spare administrator has failed, regardless of how good it is.
typicalProblems: Volunteers, not employees — availability and training currency matter and are not HR records
Training currency known by the training officer, not by the system, at the moment a coordinator needs it
Equipment custody informal
Vehicle compliance in calendar reminders
Incidents reconstructed afterwards
Donor relationship disconnected from operational reality

exampleEngagements.summary: Custom operational application for volunteers, training currency, incidents, assets and vehicles, with CRM kept for donors.

exampleEngagements.descriptor: South African early-childhood development sector organisation
exampleEngagements.summary: Training, assessment and verification lifecycle.

architectureConsiderations: The operational domain usually needs a custom application; the donor domain usually does not
Training currency should gate participation, not fill a report
Custody as a recorded event
Administrative capacity is a hard design constraint
Donor and operational data stay separate for access and privacy reasons
commonRisks: Administrative capacity below what any system needs
Register data quality at go-live
Volunteer adoption against a familiar spreadsheet
Scope growing to match ambition rather than capacity

name: Professional Services
summary: Partner-led and white-label delivery — where a significant share of my work actually happens.
overview: A large part of my work is behind an implementation partner: the end client is theirs, the brand on the document is theirs, and the commercial conversation is theirs.

What this demands is a specific discipline — client-facing material that carries no internal reasoning, no provenance, no named individuals and no change logs. Internal rigour is not evidence; it is noise to the reader.
typicalProblems: Discovery calls where the partner needs solutioning support in real time
Documents that must carry the partner's brand and tone
Internal reasoning leaking into client-facing material
Scope committed to a number before it was understood
Opportunities needing an architecture before they can be quoted
Delivery drifting from the scope it was priced against

exampleEngagements.summary: Audit of a partner's self-service scoping configurator against their own product catalogue, pricing and the meeting record.
Webhook-to-helpdesk integration architecture.
Discovery support, solution architecture, client-facing documentation and estimation support delivered under partner branding.

architectureConsiderations: Client-facing documents carry no internal material — no change logs, no provenance, no named individuals
The partner owns the client relationship and the commercial conversation
Separate the partner's commercial decisions from the project team's technical constraints
Scope protection before a number is committed
Document standards belong to the partner, not to me
commonRisks: Internal reasoning reaching the end client
Commercial decisions being made by default in a build
Scope committed before it was understood
Project-team constraints presented to the client as requirements

name: Customer Support Operations
summary: Multi-channel support as a discipline in its own right, wherever the industry.
overview: Support is where every other process shows its cracks. A support conversation is usually an order problem, a stock problem or a logistics problem wearing a different hat.
Designing support in isolation produces a faster way to fail. The work is defining what resolved means, separating the outcomes, and making the recorded path also the fast path.
typicalProblems: Four channels, some of which become records
The same customer handled by agents who cannot see each other
No definition of resolved
Priority set by whoever complains loudest
Categories grown until reporting is meaningless
Machine-created tickets with no owner

exampleEngagements.summary: Support transformation across messaging, social, email and phone with distinct outcome paths.
Automated ticket creation from external platform events.
Claims and complaint handling with cascading classification.

architectureConsiderations: Every channel creates a ticket, or the record is fiction
Classification before assignment
SLA needs a business-hours calendar and pause conditions
Evidence requirements tied to claim windows
The helpdesk links to the order system rather than copying it
commonRisks: Channel approval outside the project's control delaying go-live
Agents reverting to the channel because it is faster
Category proliferation returning
Silent integration failure destroying trust in the queue

name: Warehouse & Fulfilment
summary: Stock, movement and dispatch — where the data is only as good as the discipline behind it.
overview: Fulfilment problems are usually not fulfilment problems. They are allocation decisions made too late, addresses never validated, and adjustments made without a reason code.
A system will expose stock accuracy. It will not create it.
typicalProblems: Stock allocated at pick rather than at order, so items oversell
Adjustments with no reason code
Returns received with no disposition state
Carrier chosen by habit
Address quality assumed
Delivery inferred from a tracking status rather than confirmed

exampleEngagements.summary: Fulfilment, courier assignment and delivery confirmation as the trigger for merchant payout.

exampleEngagements.descriptor: South African B2B laboratory and instrumentation distributor
exampleEngagements.summary: Stock and price visibility surfaced to sales from the inventory platform.

architectureConsiderations: One system owns stock quantity
Allocation on confirmation, not on pick
Mandatory adjustment reason codes
Delivery confirmation as a controlled event where money depends on it
Address validation before scheduling or dispatch
commonRisks: Real-time sync assumed but constrained by API limits
Stock accuracy as a discipline problem
Reverse logistics treated like forward logistics
Carrier data quality determining whether confirmation is trustworthy
